name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٠)
```

```english
And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘What! Do you commit an outrage none in the world ever committed before you?! (80)
```

```arabic
إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُّسْرِفُونَ (٨١)
```

```english
Indeed you come to men with desire instead of women! Indeed, you are a profligate lot.’ (81)
```

```arabic
وَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَخْرِجُوهُم مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Expel them from your town! They are indeed a puritanical lot.’ (82)
```

```arabic
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٨٣)
```

```english
Thereupon We delivered him and his family, except his wife; she was one of those who remained behind. (83)
```

```arabic
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٨٤)
```

```english
Then We poured down upon them a rain [of stones]. So observe how was the fate of the guilty! (84)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ** “And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘Do you commit this indecency...’” (7:80) until the end of the verse. Its apparent meaning is that it is the conjunction (عطف) of one story upon another; that is, the conjunction of His saying: **لُوطًا** (Lot) upon **نُوحًا** (Noah) in His saying in the first story: **لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا** “Certainly We sent Noah” (7:59). Thus, the implied meaning would be: “And certainly We sent Lot when he said to his people...” However, what is customary from similar structures in the Quran is that there is an implied command “Mention” (اذكر) as indicated by the context (السياق). Accordingly, the implied meaning is: “And mention Lot, whom We sent, when he said to his people...” It appears that the change in context is due to the fact that Lot was among the prophets who followed the law (شريعة) of Abraham (عليه السلام), not the law of Noah (عليه السلام). For this reason, the context at the beginning of his story was changed from the preceding context in the stories of Noah, Hud, and Salih; the context shifted at the start of his story and then returned to the previous style in the story of Shu‘ayb (عليه السلام).

Lot—as will follow, Allah willing, in the detailed account of his story in Surah Hud—was sent to the people of Sodom and others, calling them to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — while they were polytheists and idolaters.

**And His saying:** **أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ** “Do you commit this indecency” (7:80). By “indecency” (الفاحشة), He means sodomy, as evidenced by His saying: **إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً** “Indeed you come to men with desire” (7:81). In His saying: **مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحدٍ مِنَ الْعَالَمِينَ** “...such as none of the worlds has ever done before you” (7:80), there is an indication that the history of the emergence of this heinous indecency traces back to the people of Lot. Most of what pertains to this discussion will follow in the detailed account of his story in Surah Hud.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِنْ دُونِ النِّسَاءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ** “Indeed you come to men with desire instead of women! Rather, you are a profligate people” (7:81). “Coming to men” is a metonymy (كناية) for performing that act with them. His saying **شَهْوَةً** “with desire” is a contextual clue (قرينة) for this, and His saying **مِنْ دُونِ النِّسَاءِ** “instead of women” is another clue. Furthermore, it implies that they had abandoned the way of women and sufficed themselves with men. Because they deviated from the path of primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and creation toward something else, He regarded them as transgressors who exceed all bounds, saying: **بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ** “Rather, you are a profligate people.”

Since their act was an innovated indecency that no one in all the worlds (العالمين) had preceded them in, He inquired about it using the interrogative coupled with the particle **إنّ** (indeed) which denotes emphasis (التحقيق); this conveys wonder and astonishment. The implied meaning is: “Is it truly the case that you come to men...”

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا** “The only answer of his people was that they said...” (7:82) until the end of the verse. That is, they had no answer, so they threatened him with expulsion from the town. Indeed, their saying: **أَخْرِجُوهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ** “Expel them from your town...” (7:82) is not a logical answer to Lot’s statement to them: **أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ** “Do you commit this indecency such as none... has ever done before you.” The answer to a statement in the context of a debate (المناظرة) is either to affirm it and acknowledge its truth, or to demonstrate the aspect of its invalidity. There is nothing of this in their saying: “Expel them...” Thus, placing what is not an answer in the position of an answer is a metonymy for the absence of a valid response and an indication of their foolishness.

They belittled Lot’s matter when they said: **أَخْرِجُوهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ** “Expel them from your town,” meaning that the town belongs to you and they are merely residents who do not belong to it; they keep themselves aloof from what you do and seek to be pure. Do not let their matter concern you, for they are but people with no numbers or strength.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَنْجَيْنَاهُ وَأَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ** “So We delivered him and his family, except his wife: she was one of those who remained behind” (7:83). In this is an indication that no one had believed in him except his family. Indeed, the Exalted has said in another place: **فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ** “But We did not find in it any but one house of those who had submitted” (51:36).

His saying: **كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ** “she was one of those who remained behind” means those of the people who passed away. It is a metaphorical metonymy (استعارة بالكناية) for perishing (الهلاك), and the rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَرًا ۖ فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** “And We rained down upon them a rain. So observe how was the fate of the guilty!” (7:84). The mention of “raining” in a context where one expects the mention of punishment (العذاب) indicates that the punishment was by means of it. The word “rain” (مطرًا) is used in the indefinite form to indicate the strangeness of its nature and the intensity of its effect. Allah the Exalted has explained it in another place by His saying: **وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهَا حِجَارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ مَنْضُودٍ مُسَوَّمَةً عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ ۖ وَمَا هِيَ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ بِبَعِيدٍ** “...and We rained upon it stones of shale, layer on layer, marked with your Lord, and they are not far from the wrongdoers” (11:82-83).

His saying: **فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ** “So observe how was the fate of the guilty” is an address directed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that he and his community (الأمة) may take heed.
